record.field: Kestra is an open-source, event-driven orchestration platform that lets you define and govern business-critical workflows declaratively as YAML, from code or the UI.

Kestra is an open-source, event-driven orchestration platform that lets you define and govern business-critical workflows declaratively as YAML, from code or the UI.

It unifies scheduled and event-driven automation across data pipelines, AI tasks, infrastructure and business processes behind a single declarative control plane. Workflows are written in a language-agnostic YAML interface and can run scripts in Python, Node.js, R, Go or Shell, with a rich plugin ecosystem connecting databases, cloud storage, APIs and SaaS tools. It targets data engineers, platform teams and anyone needing reliable, auditable orchestration without locking logic into application code.

Key features
  • Declarative YAML workflows defined from a built-in code editor or the UI, with Git version control integration
  • Event-driven and scheduled triggers, with retries, error handling, conditional branching and dynamic tasks
  • Real-time topology view rendering each workflow as a live Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)
  • Extensive plugin catalog to extract, transform and load data across clouds, databases and APIs, and run code in any language
  • AI Copilot to generate workflows from natural-language prompts and AI Agents for autonomous, tool-using orchestration with human-in-the-loop approvals
  • Self-hostable on Docker or Kubernetes, including air-gapped deployments; namespaces, unit tests and SLAs for production governance
Best for

Data engineering, platform and DevOps teams that need to orchestrate data, AI, infrastructure and business workflows at scale with version control, observability and full auditability — a modern, language-agnostic alternative to Python-DAG schedulers.

services: postgres: image: postgres volumes: - ./postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data environment: POSTGRES_DB: kestra POSTGRES_USER: kestra POSTGRES_PASSWORD: k3str4 healthcheck: test: - CMD-SHELL - pg_isready -d $${POSTGRES_DB} -U $${POSTGRES_USER} interval: 30s timeout: 10s retries: 10 container_name: '{{ INSTANCE_CODE }}_postgres' restart: unless-stopped networks: - internal kestra: image: kestra/kestra:latest pull_policy: always user: root command: server standalone volumes: - ./kestra_data:/app/storage - /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock - /tmp/kestra-wd:/tmp/kestra-wd environment: KESTRA_CONFIGURATION: "datasources:
 postgres:
 url: jdbc:postgresql://postgres:5432/kestra
\ \ driverClassName: org.postgresql.Driver
 username: kestra
 password: k3str4
kestra:
\ \ server:
 basicAuth:
 enabled: false
 username: "admin@localhost.dev" # it\ \ must be a valid email address
 password: k3str4p@ss
 repository:
 type: postgres
\ \ storage:
 type: local
 local:
 basePath: "/app/storage"
 queue:
 type:\ \ postgres
 tasks:
 tmpDir:
 path: /tmp/kestra-wd/tmp
 url: http://localhost:8080/
" ports: - '{{ PORT_HTTP }}:8080' depends_on: postgres: condition: service_started container_name: '{{ INSTANCE_CODE }}' restart: unless-stopped networks: - internal - agz-proxy-network networks: internal: driver: bridge agz-proxy-network: external: true

#!/bin/bash set -euo pipefail cd "$SERVICE_PATH" # Dump consistente de PostgreSQL (servicio en marcha) → $BACKUP_DATA_DIR docker compose exec -T postgres sh -c 'pg_dump -U "$POSTGRES_USER" -d "$POSTGRES_DB" --no-owner -Fc' \ > "$BACKUP_DATA_DIR/db.dump" echo "pg_dump OK ($(wc -c < "$BACKUP_DATA_DIR/db.dump") bytes)"

#!/bin/bash set -euo pipefail cd "$SERVICE_PATH" # Levanta solo la DB, espera readiness y restaura docker compose up -d postgres for i in $(seq 1 30); do docker compose exec -T postgres sh -c 'pg_isready -U "$POSTGRES_USER"' && break sleep 2 done docker compose exec -T postgres sh -c 'pg_restore -U "$POSTGRES_USER" -d "$POSTGRES_DB" --clean --if-exists --no-owner' \ < "$RESTORE_DATA_DIR/db.dump" # Verificación: la DB no quedó vacía (sh -c con dobles comillas; $VAR del contenedor con \$) N=$(docker compose exec -T postgres sh -c "psql -U \$POSTGRES_USER -d \$POSTGRES_DB -tAc \"SELECT count(*) FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_schema='public'\"") echo "tablas restauradas: $N" test "$N" -gt 0
